title: Morning Robotics Scan
report:
## Teleoperated Humanoid Surgical Robots

### The Breakthrough
Researchers at UC San Diego demonstrated that a 60‑lb humanoid robot can be teleoperated to perform laparoscopic gallbladder removal on large non‑primate mammals, matching the precision of existing surgical systems. The study showed two successful configurations: a human‑robot collaborative team and a fully robot‑robot team, both using custom adapters to mount standard surgical tools.

### The Caveats
The work provides no quantitative metrics or baseline comparisons, leaving the claimed precision advantage unverified.

## MIT Puffin‑Inspired Aerial‑Aquatic Robot

### The Breakthrough
MIT unveiled a half‑pound, bird‑sized robot with a three‑foot wingspan that flaps at 5‑16 Hz, enabling water‑to‑air launches in under a second. Its flexible nylon‑carbon‑fiber wings and waterproofed body give neutral buoyancy, allowing it to swim over a mile and fly roughly four miles on a single charge.

### The Caveats
Performance is shown only in a short video without quantitative tests, baseline comparisons, or field trials.

## FabriVLA Manipulation Model

### The Breakthrough
FabriVLA combines an InternVL3.5 vision‑language backbone with a flow‑matching action head in a single‑stage, end‑to‑end training pipeline. The 1 B‑parameter model attains a tier‑average success rate of 90 % on the Meta‑World MT50 benchmark across 50 manipulation tasks.

### The Caveats
The 90 % success rate is presented without comparison to existing MT50 baselines, so its relative advantage is unclear.

## LingBot‑VA 2.0 Video‑Action Foundation

### The Breakthrough
LingBot‑VA 2.0 introduces a semantic visual‑action tokenizer, causal pretraining, a sparse MoE backbone, and asynchronous inference that together enable high‑frequency closed‑loop control; few‑shot real‑world tests show it can generalize to complex manipulation tasks.

### The Caveats
Few‑shot results lack quantitative comparison to prior methods, leaving the claimed generalization unverified.

## ContactMimic Humanoid Contact Control

### The Breakthrough
ContactMimic augments keypoint tracking with binary contact commands and a contact‑following reward, allowing policies to deliberately make or suppress contact while preserving motion accuracy; it outperforms a keypoint‑only baseline in simulation and sim‑to‑real trials.

### The Caveats
The paper benchmarks only against a single baseline and provides no quantitative success rates.

## DexVerse Dexterous Manipulation Benchmark

### The Breakthrough
DexVerse offers 100 diverse dexterous tasks across three robot arms and six hands, visual randomization, and a VR‑based teleoperation dataset of 3,180 demonstrations; initial experiments reveal large performance gaps for state‑of‑the‑art policies.

### The Caveats
Only 19 of the 100 tasks are evaluated and no real‑hardware testing is performed.

## PCLA CARLA Evaluation Framework

### The Breakthrough
PCLA decouples 37 pretrained autonomous‑driving agents from the CARLA core, supports multi‑agent simulation on a single machine, works with any CARLA 0.9.16+ release, and includes scripts for downloading weights and handling dependencies.

### The Caveats
The framework is demonstrated solely via video and offers no quantitative benchmarks or comparisons.

## Cactus v2 On‑Device AI Runtime

### The Breakthrough
Cactus v2 adds a confidence‑based routing probe to model weights, enabling lossless 4‑bit quantization and automatic cloud fallback; it runs a Gemma‑1‑4‑E2B model at 169 tokens/s on an M5 Max with 1.3 GB RAM, matching FP16 accuracy.

### The Caveats
Only internal speed numbers are reported, with no comparison to existing edge runtimes.

### Priority
The teleoperated humanoid surgical robot showcases real‑world, high‑impact deployment of humanoid platforms in complex medical tasks, making it the most compelling story to start your day.

### One action
If you work on medical or humanoid robotics, review the Surgie system design and its tool‑adapter integration for low‑cost surgical assistance.

### What to watch
FabriVLA, LingBot‑VA 2.0, ContactMimic, and DexVerse all push toward more capable manipulation through vision‑language or video‑action models and richer benchmarks. Expect rapid iteration as researchers address the shared lack of quantitative comparisons and real‑hardware validation.
section_titles: Teleoperated Humanoid Robots Perform First Live Animal Surgeries
MIT aerial‑aquatic robot inspired by puffins flies from water to air
FabriVLA: lightweight vision‑language‑action model for precise multi‑task manipulation
LingBot-VA 2.0: Native Video‑Action Pretraining for Generalizable Robot Control
ContactMimic: Humanoid Object Interaction via Contact Control
DexVerse: Modular Benchmark for Multi-Task, Multi-Embodiment Dexterous Manipulation
PCLA: Framework for testing pretrained autonomous driving agents in CARLA
Cactus v2: On‑device AI runtime with 4‑bit quantization and confidence‑based cloud fallback
